questions.question: What ultimately limits the parallelism of one consumer group on a topic?
options: The number of useful partitions relative to the required ordering boundary.
Only the number of pods the cluster can launch.
Only the size of the event payload schema.
The number of README files in the repository.
questions.explanation: Consumer-group parallelism is bounded by partitioning, not just fleet size.

questions.question: Why is partition-key choice a correctness decision, not just a scaling decision?
options: Because the key determines which events preserve ordering together and which do not.
Because the broker requires keys to be human-readable.
Because keys eliminate the need for lag monitoring.
Because partition count never changes after launch.
questions.explanation: The key defines the ordering scope that the system can preserve.

questions.question: What is the clearest sign of a hot-partition problem?
options: One or a few partitions accumulate disproportionate lag and resource usage compared with the rest.
All partitions have identical traffic and latency.
The producer uses message IDs.
The cluster has more than one consumer group.
questions.explanation: Skew shows up as uneven lag and throughput across partitions.

questions.question: Why might adding more consumers fail to improve throughput?
options: If partition count or hot-key skew is already the real bottleneck, extra consumers stay idle or contend on the same hot partitions.
Because consumer groups cannot have more than three members.
Because offset commits disable horizontal scale.
Because replay is impossible once a second consumer joins.
questions.explanation: Extra consumers help only when there is unclaimed parallel work.

questions.question: Which metric most directly shows rebalance pain?
options: Rebalance duration and the corresponding stall or lag increase during membership changes.
Only total disk size on producer hosts.
Average source file length.
The number of markdown headings in the lesson.
questions.explanation: Rebalance pain is about stop time and recovery lag, not static resource counts.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with creating many more partitions early?
options: You gain headroom for parallelism, but you pay with more metadata, coordination, and operational overhead.
You gain more partitions and automatically eliminate hot keys.
You remove the need for consumer lag metrics.
You make repartitioning impossible forever.
questions.explanation: Higher partition counts help scale but increase management cost.

questions.question: What is the hidden risk of choosing a random partition key when per-account ordering is required?
options: Updates for the same account can be processed concurrently on different partitions and violate business expectations.
The topic can no longer be replayed.
The producer loses the ability to batch messages.
The broker refuses offset commits.
questions.explanation: Random partitioning breaks the local ordering guarantee the product needs.

questions.question: Which answer sounds most senior in a consumer-group interview?
options: I will add more workers until lag disappears.
I will pick the partition key from the ordering scope, measure skew explicitly, and discuss how rebalances affect recovery and rollout.
I will avoid discussing offsets because the broker handles them.
I will use one partition first and scale later.
questions.explanation: That answer ties correctness, scale, and operations together.
